summary: Martin Fowler explains the confusing distinction between aggregation and composition in UML. The confusion stems from pre-UML methods having different semantics for part-whole relationships, which couldn't be reconciled during standardization. Aggregation (white diamond) carries no standard meaning beyond regular association and is essentially a 'modeling placebo.' Composition (black diamond) has actual semantics, specifically that an object can only be part of one composition relationship at a time.
key_points: UML aggregation and composition confusion stems from pre-UML methods having incompatible definitions of part-whole relationships
Aggregation has no standard semantics beyond regular association - it's a 'modeling placebo'
Composition enforces that an object can only be part of one composition relationship at a time
If you encounter aggregation in a diagram, you should ask the author what they mean by it
money_quotes: Few things in the UML cause more consternation than aggregation and composition.
Aggregation has no semantics beyond that of a regular association. It is, as Jim Rumbaugh puts it, a modeling placebo.
People can, and do, use it - but there are no standard meanings for it.
If you see it, you should inquire as to what the author means by it.
I would advise not using it yourself without some form of explanation.
key_insight: UML aggregation is semantically meaningless - a 'modeling placebo' - while composition actually constrains objects to belong to only one whole at a time.
